speaker: interviewer
text: Interview May 24, 1996.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have told me that after you moved to Israel you became involved with a group of people that were involved with Schindler after the war. Can you tell us briefly about your experience with Schindler after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we realized after the war how much we owe Schindler. And we-- we met Schindler when we were in Germany. And we knew that he lost all his money, all his big money. He was left without anything. As I told you, we owe him our lives. And we knew also who Schindler was. As a person, it was very difficult to help him because he was a very special person. We didn't speak about him. He was a womanizer. He was a drinker. He was an adventurer, a man who liked to be, I should call it would like to be on the stage, would like to be acting, but always in the middle of the stage, always has the main actor-- a man who wasn't able to conduct a normal life. He liked action. He liked tension. He liked adventures. He liked, even then, just, I think, he felt good in all this. And this was not the right time after the war for him. So the first thing we did was an appeal to the Jewish Congress after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was given $25,000, which were a lot of money at that time. And he was sent with a group of Jewish friends to Argentina in order to begin with his wife a new life there. And they were told that the best investment they can make, in order to make money without working too hard, is a farm, to establish a farm, a nutria farm. You call it nutria? Food?

speaker: interviewee
text: The small animals-- to make furs. So they used the whole money to make this farm. But he neglected it in such a way, that in a few months, he was out of money, out of farm, and full of debts. And being Schindler, he left his wife, ran away, and went to the States. His wife, until to day, in Buenos Aires. He forgot her there, and he left her there. It was typical. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from one side, he was a kind of a crook, if you speak about his relations to his wife. The other side, he was working with his wife in order to save us. It was something very, very strange. So he went away to the States. He stayed in the States a few months. And he was-- he couldn't stay longer because he hadn't got the permission. He went back to Germany. He was given, again, money to establish a factory for something-- to make roofs or something like this. It was a time when Germany was rebuilding. Everybody made a fortune on this kind of producing this kind of tools. I don't know what it was exactly. He was bankrupt after half a year because he was drinking, womanizing, and so on, and was left with debts until today. Somebody pays his debts. So the last solution was to make them come to Israel every year a few months, for two or three months at least, and to send him every a month a limited amount of money in order to keep him above the water, but not giving him too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: But even this didn't succeed too much because this was a man who when he got $100, he-- he lost them in two minutes. If he couldn't drink or eat, then he-- he-- he went to a phone and called the States as long as this $100 were left. And this brings me to-- to-- to the question maybe I am always asked-- why-- what was his motivation? Why he did it? So we think-- we couldn't get the answer on it. We tried after the war. We were very motivated to get the answers of it. We-- we hosted him in all our private houses, and we tried to get out of him what was his motivation. And again, I have to-- to-- to say that Schindler's approach, positive approach to Jews, goes back to-- to the year '40, '41. That means in the time when the Germans were at the top of their success, not at the end-- at the end of the war when everybody can say now he knew that it goes down [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: He began in the time when everybody thought the Germans are conquering all the world, or Europe at least. So we-- we couldn't get the answer what he was-- what was his motivation, why he did it. We-- we think that being almost unknowable or being what he was, his own-- the only one-- that if he would be known, or if he would be a tranquil man, a thinking man, and not such an impulsive man, maybe he wouldn't do it. And at the end, I'm always quoting the words-- or the answer of this superior judge, Dr. [INAUDIBLE], who asked also many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was the leader of us, of our community, which took care of Schindler after the war. He was asked always, what was the motivation? Why you did it? So at the end he said, listen, we stopped-- we stopped asking. We stopped looking for the answer. And we say for us, what is for us important-- and-- is it that the only answer is he saved 1,200 Jews because he saved another additional 100 Jews at the end of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he saved us. Why he did it-- how he did it, we know. Why he did it, we won't know. He-- maybe he himself, he didn't know the answer. But the main thing that he is the only man in the history of the Holocaust that saved-- the only one person who saved more than 1,000 Jewish lives. And this has to be remembered. And again, what is so very important is that after the book was written by Keneally, Spielberg took over and accepted to make the film. It took him 10 years to make the film. And now we know-- I-- I-- I must say that in-- in the beginning when we heard that the film would be made, we the people, the survivors, the Schindler's List people were very afraid that it would be a Hollywood film. And you know, a kind of-- of-- of romance or something, that they will make our-- out of it something very cheap. And we were very afraid of it. And many of us didn't want the film to be made. But then we had Spielberg, and we couldn't do anything, by the way. So now we see how important it is, the film. Spielberg made something that nobody was able to make. He renewed the-- the-- the knowledge about the Holocaust. He-- I think that he won the battle, if-- if such a battle can be-- can be settled at all-- against those who are denying the Holocaust, who are-- who are diminishing the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's so important for-- for the-- for the Gentiles and for the generations that will come to see really what happened and to-- to revive the-- the-- the-- the knowledge and the-- the feelings about the Holocaust. And in the same time, to see that even in the darkest times, there can be a possibility that one individual can make something that they can swim against. Three men can do things that are not thinkable and a kind of and maybe can be also a warning signal that actually shouldn't happen again. So it would be. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all. ZIONA AUSTRIAN (DAUGHTER): I'm Ziona Austrian. And the interviewer of this interview. This has been the most special interview for me because I interviewed my father. My father, Dr. Jonathan Dresner, is visiting from Israel. I live in Lyndhurst, Ohio. And this is the picture of our family that's here. My husband is at work right now. And I have a son who is in college. And I'll let my father describe the rest of the family. And this is my wife, Helen, whom I met in Germany in '49. I met her during '49. In the same year, we came to Israel. And since then, we live there, and we are happily married there. And we have two daughters, one living in here in the States and the second one living in Israel, both married. Our daughter in the States has two children. One is standing behind me, a high school student, Karen. Second one is graduating now from Princeton University in just days. Second daughter, living in Israel, has two small children. She's English teacher. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I should say that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: ZIONA AUSTRIAN (DAUGHTER): Karen, would you tell us about the special trip you took?

speaker: interviewee
text: KAREN AUSTRIAN (GRANDDAUGHTER): About three weeks ago, I came back from the March of the Living, which is a two-week trip for Jewish teenagers, one week to Poland and one week to Israel. And it was a very emotional experience, but it was an amazing trip. And it really opened my eyes to a lot of things about the Holocaust and things about the human spirit and people in this world.

speaker: interviewer
text: ZIONA AUSTRIAN (DAUGHTER): Can you explain in one or two sentences what the March of the Living is?

speaker: interviewee
text: KAREN AUSTRIAN (GRANDDAUGHTER): It's-- the March of the Living itself is-- it's a march of-- there's 5,000 Jewish teenagers who come from all over the world on Yom Hashoah. We marched from Auschwitz to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please describe the picture and who is in it and when was it taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: This Is a picture of my father when he was maybe 20 years old or less even. So it must be in the time of the end of the First World War. My father was born in 1893. Or maybe it's even during the war. It was the only one saved-- photograph that we caught after the war from somebody who kept it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is the second from the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What's your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's first name is Juda. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Everybody says that I look like my father. And everybody who meets me and knew my father recognizes me because I am so looking like-- like my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture, and when, where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is again a picture we got after the war from somebody who kept it. This is me and my sister. I think we-- I am here five years old or something like this. And my sister must be two years-- no. No. I have-- I am six years old, and she's maybe two years old. And this is on a vacation place where we were going during the summer vacations. This is the only one picture which I have from my childhood. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And describe who we see in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- my picture. In the first month of my being a student at the German university, that means in-- from the year '45. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Please describe the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the first picture which I made with my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] girlfriend, my wife, when I met her in Germany in '47. [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a family picture. Would you please tell us who are the people in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle of the picture are my parents, my mother, my father. Above them is my brother-in-law, this cousin doctor who I was talking about in Plaszow. This is my sister, his wife. This is me and my wife. These are my two daughters. This is Ziona, and this is Shelly, living in Israel. And this are the children, these two and this one, are the of my sister and my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in-- I don't remember-- mid-'60s-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] late '60s, I think. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] This is my daughter Shelly, living in Israel. This is her husband, Ron. [PAUSES FOR 34 SECONDS] And these are the two children of my daughter Shelly and her husband, Ron. It's Tal, the girl, and Or, the boy. [PAUSES FOR 19 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Please go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my-- my daughter Ziona and her husband, Dan, on their vacation trip to Oregon. [PAUSES FOR 28 SECONDS] And this is my grandson Guy, how is graduating now from University Princeton. And he is the son of Ziona and Dan Austrian. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] And this is last, but not least, my granddaughter Karen, who is now 16, is still in high school in [INAUDIBLE] high school in Lyndhurst. [PAUSES FOR 73 SECONDS]
